title: St. John the Baptist (Monthly) — St. Tychon of Amathus & St. Kauma the Martyr
narrative:
title": "St. John the Baptist (Monthly) — St. Tychon of Amathus & St. Kauma the Martyr

narrative": "The Monthly Commemoration of St. John the Baptist the Forerunner and Prophet, St. Tychon the Bishop of Amathus the Wonderworker, and St. Kauma the Martyr

On the sixteenth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates again the monthly commemoration of the holy, glorious Prophet, Forerunner, and Baptist John — the second time his feast has returned in the month of Sene, a doubling that the tradition regards not as redundancy but as emphasis. The month that is moving now through its second half is a month that has been saturated with apostolic and prophetic witnesses, and the return of John at its midpoint is the calendar's declaration that all of these witnesses — the apostles, the philosophers, the martyrs, the desert fathers, the bishops — are oriented toward the same point to which John's finger always points: the one who comes after him, whose sandal he is not worthy to untie, who will baptise not with water but with the Holy Spirit and fire. The community that gathers around John's feast for the third time in the year is the community that has needed three times to be reminded that its life does not originate in its own virtue or its own tradition but in the one to whom John bears witness, and that this reminder cannot be given too often.

The second monthly commemoration of John in Sene falls midway through the month's second half, and the tradition places it here as the pivot between the great feasts of the apostles that have come before and the great feasts of the apostolic princes — Peter and Paul — that are approaching at the month's end. John stands between the prophets and the apostles as he always has, the hinge between the old and the new, the voice that introduces what it cannot fully contain, and his second appearance in Sene is the tradition's way of saying that even within a single month the transition from prophecy to fulfilment must be re-enacted, that the distance between the Baptist's finger and the Saviour's face is not a distance that can be crossed once and then forgotten but one that must be crossed again and again in the life of faith and in the rhythm of the liturgical calendar.

Saint Tychon of Amathus, Bishop of Amathus in Cyprus in the fifth century, is one of the great wonderworkers of the Cypriot tradition and is particularly beloved in that island's church as one of the patron saints of the vine — a patronage that arose, according to the tradition, from a miracle in which Tychon planted a dry vine-cutting and it immediately blossomed and bore fruit, a sign the tradition reads as the bishop's participation in the creative power of the one who turned water into wine at Cana. Tychon of Amathus is the bishop-wonderworker whose sanctity is expressed not primarily in theological controversy or martyrdom but in the quiet miracles of pastoral life: the miraculous vine, the healing of the sick who came to him, the conversions of pagans who encountered in him a man whose life was so evidently filled with the divine presence that their own religious certainties were shaken by proximity to it. He is the tradition's icon of the bishop as holy man, the shepherd whose holiness is itself a form of teaching.

Saint Kauma the Martyr completes the assembly of this sixteenth day of Sene as a witness whose brief but faithful testimony for the faith joins the great company of those whose names the Church preserves because faithfulness deserves to be remembered even when the record of the circumstances is sparse. Kauma died for the confession of Christ in a manner the tradition records with the brevity appropriate to a witness whose significance lies not in the drama of his death but in its quality: he was asked to deny and he would not, he was threatened and he would not yield, and the death that followed was not spectacular but was final. The tradition honours Kauma as it honours all the lesser-known martyrs — not because their stories are less important than those of the great and celebrated witnesses but because the Church's insistence on preserving their names is itself a theological act, a declaration that every faithful death is witnessed by God even when it goes unwitnessed by history, and that the Book of Life is more complete than any martyrology. Amen.
